Summary:
Systematic elaboration

Sox9 functions as an astrocyte specifier and in adults is expressed in naïve astrocytes and is upregulated in reactive astrocytes following brain or spinal cord injury. Nerve injury triggers Sox9 phosphorylation, which enhances Sox9 nuclear translocation and transcriptional activity. Phosphorylated Sox9 directly upregulates Hk1 and sustains high Hk1 activation under pathological conditions. Elevated Hk1 increases glycolytic flux in astrocytes and affects gene expression via histone lactylation. This regulatory mechanism promotes detrimental astrocyte properties in the dorsal horn and contributes to neuropathic pain (NeP) development. Phosphorylated Sox9 contributes to the emergence of an Hk1-mediated neuroinflammatory astrocyte cluster 1 and induces an Astro 2 phenotype with neurodegenerative and demyelinating properties. Upstream kinases such as PKA, MAPK, and PI3K/Akt may regulate Sox9 phosphorylation during pain pathogenesis.
References: Chen, Y., Liao, Y., Zhu, H., Wu, Z., Wan, Y., Feng, X., Fan, C., Zhan, D., Cai, H., Cheung, M., Cheung, C. W., & Liu, J. A. (2025). Sox9 regulation of hexokinase 1 controls neuroinflammatory astrocyte subtypes in a rat model of neuropathic pain. Nat Commun, 16(1), 10249. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-65092-5
